## [00:00 - 03:00]

Guys, Cole here, and we're going to cover setter and closer scorecards in this training. So specifically, we'll cover what a scorecard actually is, why it's important, why we use it. We'll go over the closer scorecard, the setter scorecard, and also how to use these to sell people on working for you. So first things first, what is a scorecard? Well, I highly recommend you read this book, Who? I forget the author off the top of my head, but it's about hiring and recruiting. And this is where I got the scorecard model from. So that's where this is inspired. But anyways, we use the scorecard to create clear agreements on the outcomes, which are the lagging indicators, that are expected by the position, along with the constituent parts that are required to achieve those outcomes, which are the leading indicators, right? Because we have leading indicators, those are the behaviors and actions we can control, lagging indicators, those are our outcomes and the things that we want. So it gets clear on both of those. First and foremost, the mission of the position, the outcomes. But then I always list out the constituent parts required to achieve those outcomes, which are also the leading indicators. So you'll see what I mean in a little bit. This not only allows us to have clear agreements of what's required and expected going into working with somebody as a team member or them coming onto our team, but it also allows us to hold them accountable in future situations, because we've already agreed on what's going to take place. So agreements are everything when working with team members. And I don't mean a contracts here. I'm not saying you have them sign a contract, all this legalese and all of this stuff. I'm talking about creating clear, like here's what the expectation is. Does that make sense? Is that something you're willing to do? And we create clear, clear agreements that look, if you're going to come on the team, here's the work and the value and the outcomes you're going to achieve in exchange for said compensation. I'm huge on full autonomy of the how and no autonomy on the what. So again, agreements and therefore, scorecards here, the way we use them, we give no autonomy on what the outcome is. We're like, here's the outcomes that this position is all about. If you come on our team, here's what the outcomes are. Those are non-negotiable. But in terms of how to achieve the outcomes, I can give a baseline set of procedures and SOPs and all of that stuff. But I want to give full autonomy on how they achieve the outcome. Because that's how you let your team members and all your employees, et cetera, innovate and actually improve the position, make it more efficient, and all of that stuff. So let's go into the Closer Scorecard first. And then we'll go into the Setter Scorecard. Now, obviously, I'm showing you guys for closers and setters here, but you could use this model for virtually any position. I mean, we use it for every single position in our company. We have a scorecard for every single position. And we update these probably every six months to a year. We don't necessarily schedule it in and make it a big event or anything. But just inevitably, I'll be talking to a team member,
